context_note.label: A note before you play
context_note.body: This data is corporate PAC giving — money the company itself distributes through its political action committee. It does not include personal donations from founders, CEOs, or board members. David Ellison's Skydance closed its takeover of Paramount Global in 2025, putting one of the major streaming networks under ownership closely aligned with the current administration. As media consolidation accelerates, the gap between corporate PAC profiles and ownership politics becomes harder to ignore.

context_note.label: A note before you play
context_note.body: This is corporate PAC giving — money the three companies' US-incorporated PACs distributed in 2024, with decisions made by US-person treasurers. It does not capture the much larger flows that actually shape platform policy: personal donations from founders and executives, lobbying spend, dark-money trade-association giving, and post-election positioning by ownership. ByteDance is headquartered in China; its US subsidiary's PAC is legal under FECA, but the corporate parent's interests are not American.

puzzles.title: Morning Fix

context_note.label: A note before you play
context_note.body: Two of these are simple — McDonald's and Starbucks each run their own corporate PACs. The third is not. Dunkin' has been owned by Roark Capital Group since 2020, a private-equity firm whose portfolio also includes Carl's Jr, Hardee's, Jimmy John's, Buffalo Wild Wings, Arby's, Auntie Anne's, Cinnabon, and Sonic. The PAC split you'll see for Dunkin' is Roark's — reflecting the political interests of a 20-brand fast-food empire, not a donut chain. Former Carl's Jr CEO Andrew Puzder was Trump's first nominee for Labor Secretary.

companies.name: Dunkin' (Roark Capital)
companies.parent_note: Donations are Roark Capital Group's corporate PAC — the private-equity owner of Dunkin' since 2020, plus Carl's Jr/Hardee's, Jimmy John's, Buffalo Wild Wings, Arby's, Auntie Anne's, Cinnabon, and Sonic.

puzzles.title: Who Spends Your Money?
puzzles.subtitle: The agencies that sat out a grant rule reveal who controls federal money.
puzzles.hook: Forty-two federal agencies signed one grant rule. The Fed and the SEC didn't. Size had nothing to do with it.
puzzles.hint: Follow the funding, not the logo.

facts.label: Reason A
facts.text: It sets interest rates for the entire economy, but it funds itself from its own operations and takes no money from Congress — so a rule about spending federal dollars simply doesn't reach it.

sources.label: Federal Reserve: not funded by congressional appropriations

facts.label: Reason B
facts.text: Its job is to police markets and punish fraud, not to administer federal financial assistance. With no aid programs to govern, it had nothing to sign.

sources.label: SEC mission: enforce the securities laws

facts.label: Reason C
facts.text: An independent, self-supporting entity funded by what you pay for stamps — Congress walled it off from this kind of executive guidance by statute.

sources.label: 39 U.S.C. § 201: an independent establishment of the executive branch

puzzles.article_hook: Forty-two federal agencies signed one grant rule — including the Marine Mammal Commission, with a budget you could lose in a rounding error. The Federal Reserve didn't sign. Neither did the SEC. Size had nothing to do with it.
puzzles.reveal: It was never about size. The Marine Mammal Commission signed because it gives out federal grants. The Federal Reserve didn't because it doesn't. One signature on this rule means one thing: you spend the public's money.

puzzles.title: The Power to Pardon
puzzles.subtitle: The Constitution hands one person the power to erase a federal crime. Match each rule to what it does.
puzzles.hook: The Constitution lets a president erase a federal crime with a signature — no judge, no appeal. There is exactly one hard limit.
puzzles.hint: Read the clause. Then read the fine print.

facts.label: Rule A
facts.text: "He shall have Power to grant Reprieves and Pardons for Offenses against the United States, except in Cases of Impeachment." One person, one signature, no appeal.

facts.label: Rule B
facts.text: It reaches only federal crimes — a pardon can't wipe out a state charge, and it can't undo an impeachment.

sources.label: Constitution Annotated — Scope of the Pardon Power

facts.label: Rule C
facts.text: The Justice Department office meant to vet every clemency request and recommend who's actually worthy — reportedly bypassed in 96% of second-term grants.

sources.label: DOJ — Office of the Pardon Attorney

facts.label: Rule D
facts.text: The Justice Department's own standard: wait at least five years after release and show you've accepted responsibility — before you even apply.

puzzles.article_hook: The Constitution lets a president erase a federal crime with a signature — no judge, no jury, no appeal. There's exactly one hard limit written into the clause itself. Everything else is just norms.
puzzles.reveal: The pardon power is the closest thing in the Constitution to a king's prerogative — one person, no appeal, and the only hard limit is impeachment. Everything else meant to keep it honest is a norm, not a law.

puzzles.title: Pardon My Receipts
puzzles.subtitle: Five federal convictions. Five pardons or commutations. Match the name to the crime they walked away from.
puzzles.hook: The Constitution lets a president erase almost any federal crime. In 2025 the pardons came fast, and the restitution vanished with them.
puzzles.hint: Follow the restitution that vanished.

facts.label: Crime A
facts.text: Founder of GPB Capital, who defrauded thousands of investors — about $1.6 billion raised in a Ponzi-like scheme. Sentenced to 7 years and $15.5M in restitution; his sentence was commuted after roughly two weeks in custody.

sources.label: NBC News — Trump commutes Gentile's sentence (Dec 2025)

facts.label: Crime B
facts.text: Ran what was then the largest Medicare fraud in U.S. history — $205 million billed, $87.5M in restitution. A 50-year sentence, commuted to time served.

facts.label: Crime C
facts.text: Raised more than $70,000 to honor fallen police officers, then spent it on rent, plastic surgery, and a wedding. Convicted of wire fraud; pardoned before being sentenced.

sources.label: DOJ — Las Vegas councilwoman convicted of charity fraud
CBS News — Trump pardons Michele Fiore (Apr 2025)

facts.label: Crime D
facts.text: A Virginia sheriff who sold auxiliary deputy badges for more than $75,000 in bribes. Convicted on all 12 counts; pardoned hours before he was to report to prison.

sources.label: CBS News — sheriff convicted of $75,000 in bribes

facts.label: Crime E
facts.text: Nikola's founder, convicted of defrauding investors of hundreds of millions with false claims about his trucks. Pardoned weeks after he and his wife gave $1.8 million to Trump's campaign.

puzzles.article_hook: The Constitution lets a president erase almost any federal crime — no judge, no jury, no appeal. In 2025 the pardons came fast, and the restitution vanished with them. Here's who walked.
puzzles.reveal: Five federal convictions. Five grants of clemency. The Constitution makes the pardon power nearly absolute — so the only real check is who's paying attention. Whether these pardons were bought is the open question; the pardons themselves are on the record.

puzzles.title: Under New Management
puzzles.subtitle: CBS sold. MSNBC renamed. TikTok under new owners. Warner Bros. on the block. Can you put the takeover wave in order?
puzzles.hook: In under a year, CBS changed hands, MSNBC lost its name, TikTok's U.S. operation got new part-owners, and the company behind HBO and CNN agreed to a $110.9 billion sale — to the same family that bought CBS.
puzzles.hint: The family deal came first — the courtroom came last.

events.title: Skydance closes the Paramount merger
events.description: David Ellison's Skydance completes its merger with Paramount Global — CBS, Paramount Pictures, and Paramount+ come under Ellison-family control.

sources.label: SEC Form 8-K12B — merger completed, filed Aug 7, 2025
Paramount sets Aug 7 close date — The Desk

events.title: MSNBC leaves NBC — and its name behind
events.description: Comcast completes the Versant spin-off: MSNBC rebrands as MS NOW and exits the NBC family, taking CNBC and the cable networks with it.

events.title: TikTok gets American part-owners
events.description: ByteDance closes the deal creating a U.S. joint venture for TikTok — Oracle, Silver Lake, and Abu Dhabi's MGX take 15% each; ByteDance keeps 19.9%.

sources.label: TikTok signs U.S. joint-venture deal — ABC News
JV set to close in January; Oracle/Silver Lake/MGX — Variety
TikTok statement on the joint venture — Bloomberg

events.title: Warner Bros. Discovery agrees to sell
events.description: After Netflix withdraws its rival bid, Warner Bros. Discovery — HBO, CNN, the Warner studio — signs a definitive $110.9 billion agreement with Paramount Skydance.

sources.label: Announcement as filed with the SEC — Feb 27, 2026
WBD signs merger agreement — NBC News

events.title: A federal judge pauses the Warner deal
events.description: With twelve state attorneys general suing to block the acquisition, a federal judge halts the Paramount Skydance-Warner deal for at least 14 days.

sources.label: California AG — court pauses the merger (oag.ca.gov)
Colorado AG — order pausing the merger (coag.gov)
Judge pauses the deal, July 20 — Motley Fool

puzzles.article_hook: In under a year, CBS changed hands, MSNBC lost its name, TikTok's U.S. operation got new part-owners, and the company behind HBO and CNN agreed to a $110.9 billion sale — to the same family that bought CBS. The order is the story. Can you rebuild it?

puzzles.title: Power Grab
puzzles.subtitle: OMB rewrote the rules for $1.1 trillion in federal grants. Match each change to what it does.
puzzles.hook: The biggest rewrite of how federal grant money works in over a decade. Critics call it a power grab. Match each change to what it actually does.
puzzles.hint: Follow the power, not the press release.

facts.label: Change A
facts.text: Before any discretionary grant goes out, a senior political appointee must sign off — and the rule bars them from simply deferring to the expert peer reviewers. Politics gets the last word over the science.

sources.label: Federal Register — Regulation for Federal Financial Assistance (91 FR 32198)

facts.label: Change B
facts.text: Agencies could cancel an active grant whenever they decide it no longer fits "agency priorities" or "the national interest" — no finding of wrongdoing, and the rule says they need not offer hearings or appeals.

facts.label: Change C
facts.text: The rulebook used to call itself flexible "guidance," not binding law. This rewrite swaps in "regulations" across hundreds of provisions — making the terms of $1.1 trillion in grants binding, without a vote in Congress.

sources.label: Federal Register — guidance reissued as regulation

facts.label: Change D
facts.text: Federal awards may no longer fund what the rule calls "gender ideology" or DEI activities — and falling short is treated as a "material breach" that can trigger termination.

puzzles.article_hook: It's the biggest rewrite of how federal grant money works in over a decade — $1.1 trillion a year, across 40-plus agencies. Critics call it a power grab: it hands whoever holds the White House new control over who gets the money. You can comment until July 13.
puzzles.reveal: Four changes, one direction: more discretionary control over federal money for whoever's in charge — flexible guidance rewritten as binding law, without a vote in Congress. It's still a proposed rule. Until July 13, the public record is open. Speak now.

puzzles.title: The Docket
puzzles.subtitle: Five milestones in Georgia's racketeering case against Donald Trump and his allies. Can you put the docket in order?
puzzles.hook: Indictment, mugshot, plea deals, a fight over the prosecutor. Put five milestones of Georgia's RICO case in the order they happened.
puzzles.hint: The charges came first and the fight over the prosecutor came last — the plea deals fell in between.

events.title: The grand jury indictment
events.description: A Fulton County grand jury charges Trump and 18 allies under Georgia's RICO Act over efforts to overturn the 2020 election.

sources.label: Georgia election racketeering prosecution — overview & sources

events.title: Trump surrenders at the Fulton County Jail
events.description: Trump turns himself in and is booked, producing the first-ever booking photo of a former U.S. president.

sources.label: Georgia election racketeering prosecution — overview & sources

events.title: Jenna Ellis pleads guilty
events.description: The campaign attorney pleads guilty and agrees to cooperate — the fourth co-defendant to strike a deal in the case.

sources.label: Georgia election racketeering prosecution — overview & sources

events.title: Judge McAfee's ruling on the Willis–Wade relationship
events.description: Judge Scott McAfee rules Willis can stay only if special prosecutor Nathan Wade steps aside; Wade resigns within hours.

sources.label: Georgia election racketeering prosecution — overview & sources

events.title: Court of Appeals disqualifies Willis
events.description: The Georgia Court of Appeals removes DA Fani Willis and her office over the appearance of a conflict of interest.

sources.label: Appeals court disqualifies Fani Willis — NBC News

puzzles.article_hook: A historic indictment, a mugshot, cascading plea deals, and a fight over the prosecutor herself — the Fulton County case moved fast, then unraveled. The order tells the story. Can you rebuild the timeline?

puzzles.title: Behind the Bench
puzzles.subtitle: Georgia Supreme Court, 2026. The race is officially nonpartisan — the endorsements aren't.
puzzles.hook: Georgia Supreme Court, 2026. The race is officially nonpartisan — the endorsements aren't.
puzzles.hint: Follow the endorsements, not the ballot label.

facts.label: Fact A · Background
facts.text: Former Georgia State Senator, representing District 6 as a Democrat.

facts.label: Fact B · Partisanship Rating
facts.text: Rated "Strong Republican" in Ballotpedia's 2020 study of judicial partisanship before joining the court.

facts.label: Fact C · Endorsements
facts.text: Endorsements include EMILY's List, Fair Fight Action, Reproductive Freedom for All, and the Democratic Party of Georgia.

facts.label: Fact D · Endorsement
facts.text: Endorsed by Frontline Policy, a Christian advocacy group whose stated mission is "a God-honoring Georgia."
